meta.name: IES-R Impact of Event Scale - Revised
meta.description: The IES-R is a 22-item self-report measure that assesses subjective distress caused by traumatic events. It measures three dimensions of post-traumatic stress: intrusion, avoidance, and hyperarousal.
meta.author: Weiss & Marmar (1997)
meta.reference: Weiss DS, Marmar CR. The Impact of Event Scale-Revised. In: Wilson JP, Keane TM, eds. Assessing Psychological Trauma and PTSD. New York: Guilford Press; 1997:399-411.
meta.instruction: Below is a list of difficulties people sometimes have after stressful life events. Please read each item and then indicate how distressing each difficulty has been for you during the past 7 days with respect to the event you experienced.

questions.text: Any reminder brought back feelings about it
I had trouble staying asleep
Other things kept making me think about it
I felt irritable and angry
I avoided letting myself get upset when I thought about it or was reminded of it
I thought about it when I did not mean to
I felt as if it had not happened or was not real
I stayed away from reminders about it
Pictures about it popped into my mind
I was jumpy and easily startled
I tried not to think about it
I was aware that I still had a lot of feelings about it, but I did not deal with them
My feelings about it were kind of numb
I found myself acting or feeling like I was back at that time
I had trouble falling asleep
I had waves of strong feelings about it
I tried to remove it from my memory
I had trouble concentrating
Reminders of it caused me to have physical reactions, such as sweating, trouble breathing, nausea, or a pounding heart
I had dreams about it
I felt watchful and on guard
I tried not to talk about it

ranges.label: Normal Range
ranges.description: Your score is within the normal range, suggesting that the event has not caused significant ongoing psychological distress. This indicates that you are not currently showing significant post-traumatic stress reactions, and your psychological recovery is progressing well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining healthy coping strategies
Allow yourself to process the experience at your own pace
Stay connected with supportive people in your life
Continue maintaining your good psychological health
Stay emotionally connected with friends and family

ranges.label: Mild Impact
ranges.description: Your score suggests some degree of post-traumatic distress that may warrant attention. Mild trauma-related reactions are normal after experiencing stressful events and typically diminish over time.
suggestions: Monitor your symptoms over the coming weeks
Practice stress management and self-care routines
Talk to someone you trust about your experiences
Consider consulting a mental health professional if symptoms persist
Allow yourself time and space to process your emotions

ranges.label: Moderate Impact
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate post-traumatic distress. The event is having a notable impact on your well-being. At this level, trauma responses may include flashbacks, avoidance behaviors, or emotional numbness that affect daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Seek support from a mental health professional
Practice grounding and relaxation techniques
Maintain regular routines for sleep and daily activities
Avoid isolating yourself; stay connected with others
Consider consulting a professional trained in trauma therapy

ranges.label: Severe Impact
ranges.description: Your score indicates severe post-traumatic distress significantly affecting your psychological well-being and daily functioning. Your trauma-related reactions are quite pronounced and may be significantly impacting your functioning. Professional trauma-focused support is recommended.
suggestions: Please seek professional help from a trauma-specialized therapist
If you are in crisis, contact a crisis helpline (e.g., 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline)
Create a safety plan for managing intense distress
Follow professional treatment recommendations closely
Contact a trauma therapy specialist as soon as possible

intrusion.label: Low
intrusion.description: Intrusion symptoms are within the normal range. This indicates that you are not currently showing significant post-traumatic stress reactions, and your psychological recovery is progressing well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your good psychological health
Stay emotionally connected with friends and family
Focus on personal growth and positive experiences
Remember to seek support if you face difficult events in the future

intrusion.label: Mild
intrusion.description: Some intrusive thoughts, memories, or dreams related to the event. Mild trauma-related reactions are normal after experiencing stressful events and typically diminish over time.
suggestions: Allow yourself time and space to process your emotions
Share your feelings with someone you trust
Maintain routines and structure in your daily life
Practice grounding techniques (such as focusing on your five senses) to stay present
Avoid repeated exposure to trauma-related information or content

intrusion.label: Moderate
intrusion.description: Notable intrusive symptoms that may disrupt daily life. At this level, trauma responses may include flashbacks, avoidance behaviors, or emotional numbness that affect daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consider consulting a professional trained in trauma therapy
Trauma-Focused CBT (TF-CBT) or EMDR may be helpful
Build a sense of safety and daily routine
Learn emotional regulation and coping skills
Avoid using alcohol or substances to cope

intrusion.label: Severe
intrusion.description: Severe intrusion symptoms significantly impacting functioning. Your trauma-related reactions are quite pronounced and may be significantly impacting your functioning. Professional trauma-focused support is recommended.
suggestions: Contact a trauma therapy specialist as soon as possible
Process traumatic memories in a safe environment
Develop a safety plan and crisis coping strategies
Ensure you have a supportive social network
Follow professional guidance for a gradual recovery process

avoidance.label: Low
avoidance.description: Avoidance symptoms are within the normal range. This indicates that you are not currently showing significant post-traumatic stress reactions, and your psychological recovery is progressing well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your good psychological health
Stay emotionally connected with friends and family
Focus on personal growth and positive experiences
Remember to seek support if you face difficult events in the future

avoidance.label: Mild
avoidance.description: Some avoidance of reminders related to the event. Mild trauma-related reactions are normal after experiencing stressful events and typically diminish over time.
suggestions: Allow yourself time and space to process your emotions
Share your feelings with someone you trust
Maintain routines and structure in your daily life
Practice grounding techniques (such as focusing on your five senses) to stay present
Avoid repeated exposure to trauma-related information or content

avoidance.label: Moderate
avoidance.description: Notable avoidance patterns that may limit activities. At this level, trauma responses may include flashbacks, avoidance behaviors, or emotional numbness that affect daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consider consulting a professional trained in trauma therapy
Trauma-Focused CBT (TF-CBT) or EMDR may be helpful
Build a sense of safety and daily routine
Learn emotional regulation and coping skills
Avoid using alcohol or substances to cope

avoidance.label: Severe
avoidance.description: Severe avoidance significantly restricting daily life. Your trauma-related reactions are quite pronounced and may be significantly impacting your functioning. Professional trauma-focused support is recommended.
suggestions: Contact a trauma therapy specialist as soon as possible
Process traumatic memories in a safe environment
Develop a safety plan and crisis coping strategies
Ensure you have a supportive social network
Follow professional guidance for a gradual recovery process

hyperarousal.label: Low
hyperarousal.description: Hyperarousal symptoms are within the normal range. This indicates that you are not currently showing significant post-traumatic stress reactions, and your psychological recovery is progressing well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your good psychological health
Stay emotionally connected with friends and family
Focus on personal growth and positive experiences
Remember to seek support if you face difficult events in the future

hyperarousal.label: Mild
hyperarousal.description: Some hyperarousal symptoms such as difficulty sleeping or irritability. Mild trauma-related reactions are normal after experiencing stressful events and typically diminish over time.
suggestions: Allow yourself time and space to process your emotions
Share your feelings with someone you trust
Maintain routines and structure in your daily life
Practice grounding techniques (such as focusing on your five senses) to stay present
Avoid repeated exposure to trauma-related information or content

hyperarousal.label: Moderate
hyperarousal.description: Notable hyperarousal affecting daily functioning. At this level, trauma responses may include flashbacks, avoidance behaviors, or emotional numbness that affect daily life and relationships.
suggestions: Consider consulting a professional trained in trauma therapy
Trauma-Focused CBT (TF-CBT) or EMDR may be helpful
Build a sense of safety and daily routine
Learn emotional regulation and coping skills
Avoid using alcohol or substances to cope

hyperarousal.label: Severe
hyperarousal.description: Severe hyperarousal symptoms. Your trauma-related reactions are quite pronounced and may be significantly impacting your functioning. Professional trauma-focused support is recommended.
suggestions: Contact a trauma therapy specialist as soon as possible
Process traumatic memories in a safe environment
Develop a safety plan and crisis coping strategies
Ensure you have a supportive social network
Follow professional guidance for a gradual recovery process
